ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Szechuan dry-fried green beans as a composite dish. Closest match is cooked green bean; the full dish includes minced pork, preserved mustard greens, dried chilies, and aromatics.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GJ covers cooked green beans as a base ingredient. No composite Szechuan dish code exists in FoodEx2; adapted from the primary vegetable component.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Szechuan composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of Dry-fried Green Beans is approximately 200 g, typically shared as part of a multi-dish Chinese meal alongside rice.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Green beans, snap, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt (FDC ID 169961) and Pork, ground, raw (FDC ID 168351); composite dish values estimated from ingredient proportions and cooking-method adjustments
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Szechuan dry-fried green beans as a composite dish. Nutrient values were estimated using USDA FoodData Central data for cooked green beans (FDC ID 169961) and ground pork (FDC ID 168351) as primary components, adjusted for the dry-frying / stir-frying cooking method (increased fat absorption from oil), and for the high-sodium contribution of preserved mustard greens (Ya Cai) and soy sauce. Input JSON values (confidence 0.9, ai_generated) were cross-checked against these USDA component values and retained where consistent. Vitamin B12 (0.1 mcg/100g) is plausible given the minced pork content. Sodium at 420 mg/100g is consistent with preserved vegetable and soy sauce contributions.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; green beans / leguminous vegetables analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Szechuan dry-fried green beans as a composite dish. GI estimated at 30 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for green beans / snap beans (GI ~30, low category). The addition of oil, minced pork, and preserved vegetables does not materially raise the GI; fat and protein slow gastric emptying, maintaining a low GI. Glycemic load per 200 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (30 × 7.2 × 2.0) / 100 = 4.32, rounded to 4.3.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from preserved mustard greens (Ya Cai) and soy sauce at 420 mg per 100 g (840 mg per standard 200 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium content (840 mg per serving, approximately 37% of the 2,300 mg daily limit); if consumed, request reduced preserved vegetable and soy sauce quantities and pair with low-sodium foods throughout the day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~30) and high dietary fibre (3.5 g/100g) from green beans support stable postprandial blood glucose
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dry-fried green beans are suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions due to their low GI (~30), high fibre content, and low net carbohydrate load (GL ~4.3 per 200 g serving); however, the elevated fat content from dry-frying should be factored into total daily energy intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated total fat (9.8 g/100g) from deep-frying / stir-frying in oil, combined with high sodium (420 mg/100g) from preserved vegetables and soy sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish cautiously: the high sodium content (840 mg per 200 g serving) contributes to blood pressure load, and the fat from frying oil adds to daily saturated and unsaturated fat intake; choose smaller portions and request preparation with minimal oil and reduced preserved vegetables.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Green beans provide vitamins K and C; garlic and ginger contribute anti-inflammatory phytochemicals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Green beans, garlic, and ginger in this dish offer anti-inflammatory micronutrients; however, the use of refined vegetable oils for frying may be mildly pro-inflammatory for some individuals with rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dry-fried Green Beans (干煸四季豆, Gān biān sìjì dòu) is a classic Szechuan Chinese vegetable dish originating from Sichuan Province, China. The defining technique — 'gan bian' (dry-frying) — involves deep-frying or stir-frying fresh green beans in hot oil with minimal moisture until their skins blister and wrinkle, concentrating their natural sweetness and creating a slightly chewy, tender-crisp texture. The blistered beans are then tossed with minced pork, dried red chilies, Szechuan peppercorns, garlic, ginger, and Ya Cai (Yibin preserved mustard greens) or Zha Cai (preserved mustard stems), along with soy sauce. The result is a savory, salty, mildly spicy, and umami-rich dish with complex layered flavors from the preserved vegetables and aromatics. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 135 kcal, 4.5 g protein, 9.8 g fat, and 7.2 g carbohydrate, with 3.5 g dietary fibre and 420 mg sodium. The high sodium content from preserved mustard greens and soy sauce makes this dish unsuitable for individuals on sodium-restricted diets or those managing hypertension. The low glycemic index (~30) and high fibre content make it appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions. The dish is not vegetarian due to the minced pork component and contains soy allergens. It is typically served as a shared side dish alongside steamed rice in a multi-course Chinese meal.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry covers Szechuan dry-fried green beans as a composite dish. Nutrient values were estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data (cooked green beans and ground pork) adjusted for cooking method and high-sodium condiments. Input JSON values (ai_generated, confidence 0.9) were cross-checked and retained where consistent with USDA component benchmarks. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for green beans. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data (FDC ID 169961 cooked green beans; FDC ID 168351 ground pork) adjusted for dry-frying oil absorption and high-sodium preserved vegetable contributions. No exact approved-source composite dish entry exists. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: green beans and pork in typical quantities contribute negligible vitamin D; confirmed near-zero per USDA FoodData Central component data. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0: vegetable oils used in stir-frying contain negligible trans fats; confirmed near-zero per USDA FoodData Central. 4. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish as prepared is not available in USDA FoodData Central or any other approved source as of 2026-04-16. 5. saturated_fat_g (1.8 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (4.2 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (3.1 g), and cholesterol_mg (8.0 mg) are estimated from USDA component data for vegetable oil and ground pork proportions; these are approximate. 6. The input JSON contained two identical duplicate portion entries (both '1 serving (200g)', 200g, is_default: true); deduplicated to a single 200 g serving. 7. The input source (Chinese Nutrition Society, cnsoc.org) is not on the approved source list and has not been used as primary nutrient evidence; USDA FoodData Central is used instead. 8. Halal status is marked false as Ya Cai preparation and pork content make halal certification uncertain without explicit certification.
